coredata.dc:title: Detection of Baicalin capsule and Scutellariae Radix based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a fluorescence probe
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Weiqing
Wu, Shuihua
Wang, Daifang
Lou, Yongming
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared via hydrothermal synthesis method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials. The synthesized N-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and multi-spectroscopic techniques. A fluorescent sensor based on the inner filter effect (IFE) was successfully established using synthesized carbon dots to detect the content of Baicalin in Baicalin capsule and the quality of Scutellariae Radix. The Baicalin linear range of the method is 1.6–72 μg·mL−1 (r = 0.9992), the detection limit is 1.0 μg·mL−1 (3-foldσ), and the precision was 0.2%. Furthermore, the N-CDs probe was used to evaluate quality of Scutellariae Radix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CDs
Scutellariae Radix
Baicalin
Fluorescence quenching

Detection of Baicalin capsule and RA by N-CDs
N-CDs PL response to Baicalin and its derivatives
Detection of Baicalin capsule and SR based on N-CDs
Influences of reaction time, pH and coexisting substances
Determination of Baicalin content in Baicalin capsules

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structures of Baicalin, Baicalein, Wogonoside, and Wogonin.
Scheme of synthesis process of N-CDs and mechanism of fluorescence quenching induced by Baicalein or its derivatives.

Characterization of N-CDs. (A) Morphology characterization by TEM analysis. (B) Fourier transform infrared spectrum. (C) Absorption spectrum and Fluorescence spectrum, λex = 358, slip = 10 nm, 420 V. Ex: excitation spectrum, Em: emission spectrum.
Fluorescence spectra of Baicalin, Baicalein, Wogonoside and Wogonin, and the fluorescence quenching with N-CDs (λex = 358 nm, λem = 453 nm).
Time stability and acid-base stability of the fluorescence quenching system (λex = 358 nm, λem = 453 nm).

Variation of fluorescence spectra of N-CDs with increasing concentration of Baicalin. From a to l, the volume of 0.9649 mg· mL−1 Baicalin in ethanol solution was 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200 and 225 μL, respectively, and the concentration of Baicalin in 3 mL of N-CDs solution was 0, 1.0682, 3.2164, 6.4328.16.082, 24.123, 32.164, 40.205, 48.246, 56.287, 64.328 and 72.369 mg·mL−1, respectively.
HPLC analysis of Baicalin (D-1, D-2) and Baicalin capsule (S-1, S-2). Solid phase: ODS, mobile phase: methanol–water-phosphoric acid (47:53:0.2), λ = 280 nm.

Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs with increasing concentration of reference Chinese herbal SR. From a to q, the volume of 3.024 mg·mL−1 Baicalin ethanol solution was 5, 25, 40, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200, 225, 250, 275, 300, 325 and 350 μL, respectively, then the concentration of Baicalin in 3 mL of N-CDs solution was 5.22, 26.12, 41.79, 52.23, 78.35, 104.57, 130.58, 156.7, 182.82 and 208.93 μg·mL−1, respectively.
Effects of coexisting foreign substances on N-CDs.
Determination of Baicalin in Baicalin capsule.
Determination of Herbal Scutellaria Baicalensis using fluorescence quenching method.

head.ce:title: Detection of Baicalin capsule and Scutellariae Radix based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a fluorescence probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Vocational College of Bioengineering, Fuzhou, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Vocational College of Bioengineering, Fuzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Institute for Food and Drug Quality Control, Fuzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Institute for Food and Drug Quality Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Institute for Food and Drug Quality Control, Fuzhou, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared via hydrothermal synthesis method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials. The synthesized N-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and multi-spectroscopic techniques. A fluorescent sensor based on the inner filter effect (IFE) was successfully established using synthesized carbon dots to detect the content of Baicalin in Baicalin capsule and the quality of Scutellariae Radix. The Baicalin linear range of the method is 1.6–72 μg·mL−1 (r = 0.9992), the detection limit is 1.0 μg·mL−1 (3-foldσ), and the precision was 0.2%. Furthermore, the N-CDs probe was used to evaluate quality of Scutellariae Radix.

ce:section.ce:para: Scutellariae Radix (SR), Scutellaria baicalensis, a perennial herb in Lamiaceae, of which the dry roots were used as a common bulk Chinese medicine. Based on the Traditional Chinese Medicine theory, SR has many functions including clearing away heat, dryness, dampness, purging fire and detoxifying. Many studies have shown that SR functioned in antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, antitumor and antidiabetes [1,2]. The main active components of SR are flavonoids, such as Baicalin, Wogonoside, Baicalein and Wogonin, their chemical structures are similar to each other (Fig. 1 ). Baicalin capsule, containing Baicalin as a main component, is used for adjuvant treatment of acute and chronic hepatitis and prolonged hepatitis. The HPLC analysis is the most representative method for determining the composition of baicalin [3]. However, the method would pollut the environment due to using the organic solvent as mobile phase and is relatively time-consuming. Furthermore, according to the Chinese Pharmacopoeia 2020 edition, the content of Baicalin in SR is required ≥9%, which only be used to evaluate whether the quality of SR is qualified, but could not identified the high-quality SR. In order to improve the method for SR quality control, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as a fluorescence probe was assigned.

Carbon dots (CDs), a nanomaterial with carbon as the main element and particle size <10 nm, have been widely used in material trace detection as biosensors, biomarkers, and photocatalysts due to their excellent properties, such as simple preparation, easy surface modification, excellent optical properties, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility [4-11]. CDs have been used as fluorescent probes to successfully detect samples, such as protein, pesticide residue and metal ions [12–14]. However, few studies have detected medicine using the fluorescent probes in the present [15,16].

To further expand the application of CDs in medicine detection, a nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was used as a fluorescent probe to determine of Baicalin and SR (Fig. 2 ) in this study. N-CDs prepared by hydrothermal synthesis of citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) [17], have higher quantum yield compared to the CDs [18], with strong emission at 453 nm upon excitation at 358 nm. After the addition of the Baicalin or the extract of SR, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs was linear quenched in a certain range, and the fluorescence quenching system was constructed, followed by the system was used to evaluate the quality of Baicalin capsule and SR, and the results were satisfactory.

CA and EDA were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Elements Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, As5+, Hg2+, Cu2+ were obtained from National Center of Analysis and Testing for Non-ferrous Metals and Electronic Materials (Beijing, China). All other reagents used in this study were of analytical grade, and ultrapure water was secondary water.

Baicalin (110715–201619, 93.5%), Baicalein (111595–201808), Wogonoside (112002–201702), and Wogonin (111514–201706) standards were purchased from National Institutes for Food and Drug Control (Beijing, China), and each of them was prepared to 1 mg·mL−1 stock solution with 70% ethanol.

Baicalin capsule (2009005, Specification: 0.25 g/tablet) was from Dongguan Jinmeiji Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd (Guangdong, China). 0.02 g of capsule granules were placed in a 250 mL bottle, followed by adding 100 mL of 70% ethanol to dissolve Baicalin by ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. The solution was cooled at room temperature, added with 70% ethanol to 250 mL, well shaken and filtered using microporous filter membrane (0.22 μm). Subsequently, the Baicalin capsule sample solution was obtained.

The reference Chinese herbal SR (120955–201810), from National Institutes for Food and Drug Control (Beijing China), was cut into pieces, finely weighed for 0.3 g, mixed with 100 mL of 70% ethanol and then weighed. After heating to reflux at 85 ℃ for 4 h, the solution of Reference Chinese herbal SR was cooled, compensated weight, filtered using microporous filter membrane (0.22 μm), and finally obtained with a concentration of 3 mg·mL−1 (1 mL of the solution was equivalent to 3 mg of the reference crude drug). SR was obtained from Fujian Baisheng Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd (Fuzhou, Fujian), and its solution was prepared to a concentration of Cx using above described method.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on 2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). PL spectra were recorded on Molecular fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian Cary EcLipse, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectra were recorded on infrared spectrophotometer (Thermofield 330-FTIR, USA). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were recorded on TECNAI G2 F20 (FEI, USA).

The high quantum yield could be obtained when N-CDs was synthesized by CA and EDA, which were usually used as pharmaceutic excipients of medicine according to the fourth part of the Chinese Pharmacopoeia. Using CA and EDA from pharmaceutic excipients to synthesize N-CDs, could avoid interference from other ingredients in medicine when detected by N-CDs. CA (18 g) and ethylenediamine (3 g) were dissolved in 50 mL of water in a 100 mL teflon reactor, followed by adding NaOH solution to adjust the pH to 5.5. The solution was transferred to the oven for reaction at 180 ℃ for 6 h, and cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 r min−1 for 20 min. The supernatant was dialyzed using dialysis bag (8000–14,000 D, USA) until the ion detection value <5, filtered by a filter membrane (0.22 μm), and stored at 4 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs PL response to Baicalin and its derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: 200 μL of N-CDs solution as a fluorescence probe was placed into each of five 5 mL centrifuge tubes, followed by adding with stock solutions (100 μL) of Baicalin, Baicalein, Wogonoside and Wogonin, respectively, then each solution was added with ultrapure water to a volume of 3 mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Baicalin capsule and RA by N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The slit widths of PL specctra were set at 10 nm. Upon excitation at 358 nm PL emission was collected in the range of 360–600 nm.

The procedure of Baicalin/BA detection was below: 200 μL of N-CDs solution and 2800 μL of distilled water were placed in a 5 mL centrifuge tubes and the PL intensity (setting as F0) was recorded. 200 μL of N-CDs solution and appropriate concentration of Baicalin/the extract from SR were added into the centrifuge tubes and diluted with distilled water to a volume of 3 mL, followed by the PL intensity (Fx) was recorded immediately. A series of Fx values were obtained via concentration change of Baicalin / the extract from SR.
The N-CDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 3 A). The carbon dots were spherical or quasispherical with uniform particle size, and the main diameter range was 5–8 nm with good dispersion.

The surface component of N-CDs was investigated by infrared spectrogram (Fig. 3B). Compared to the raw materials of CA and EDA, the broad peak at 3400 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H, the peak at 1690 cm−1 was the stretching vibration peak of CO, the peak at 1580 cm−1 was the in-plane bending vibration peak of N–H, the peaks at 1400 cm−1were the C-N stretching vibration peak and O–H in-plane bending vibration peak, respectively, and the peak at 1190 cm−1 was the stretching vibration peak of C-N. These results indicated that the surface of N-CDs contains many oxygen-containing groups and a certain number of amino groups.

N-CDs had a wide absorption region in the UV–visible region and a characteristic absorption peak at 340 nm (Fig. 3C). In the fluorescence spectrum, the emission spectrum (Em) of N-CDs excited at 358 nm had a maximum emission wavelength of 453 nm, the excitation spectrum obtained from emission at 453 nm had a maximum excitation wavelength of 358 nm (slip = 10 nm, 420 V). The 15-fold diluted N-CDs solution showed blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation light from 365 nm UV lamp but not sunlight. The N-CDS solution was stable after stored at −4 ℃ for 3 months.

Based on above results, it could be infered that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs may be due to inner filter effect (IFE) [19]. The maximum excitation wavelength of N-CDs was 358 nm, which had a large overlap with the maximum UV absorption wavelength (340 nm). Therefore, when both are present, Baicalin would absorb the excitation light from the carbon point, which leads to fluorescence quenching of N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs PL response to Baicalin and its derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: When N-CDs was mixed with Baicalin, Baicalein, Wogonoside and Wogonin, the effect of each substance on N-CDs fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 4 ). Baicalin, Baicalein, Wogonoside, and Wogonin belong to flavonoid and are similar to each other in structure. All of them showed fluorescences (λex = 358 nm) which had a shoulder peak and a wave peak at 413 and 435 nm, respectively. After mixed with N-CDS, the fluorescence peaks disappeared at 413 and 435 nm, and the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs were quenched to varying degrees, but there were no obvious red shifts or blue shifts in the position of N-CDs fluorescence peaks. The fluorescence quenching system using N-CDs as fluorescent probes was established, which was used to determine the content of Baicalin and its derivatives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Baicalin capsule and SR based on N-CDs
Influences of reaction time, pH and coexisting substances
ce:section.ce:para: To test fluorescence quenching system available for Baicalin / SR detection, the influence factors, such as reaction time, pH and coexisting substances, were took into account.

At a time interval of 30 min within 3 h, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and fluorescence quenching systems containing Baicalin, Baicalein, Wogonoside and Wogonin were stable with ⊿F (⊿F = F0 − Fx) unchanged (Fig. 5 A). The results indicated that a test can be quickly conducted after adding Baicalin or extract of SR in the system and shaking well without time-consuming.

The pH value of the N-CDs solution was adjusted by HCl or NaOH solutions. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was stable in the pH range of 6–10, and decreased when pH was <6 (Fig. 5B). The results indicated that the quality of Baicalin capsule and SR could be determined in the pH range of 6–10.

Pharmaceutic excipients and metal ions, coexisted in Baicalin capsule and SR, were added into fluorescence quenching system, respectively. According to Chinese Pharmacopoeia, the upper limit contents of heavy metal ions Pb2+, Cd2+, As5+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ were 5 mg·kg−1, 1 mg·kg−1, 2 mg·kg−1, 0.2 mg·kg−1 and 20 mg·kg−1, respectively. Based on the sample weight, the high limit of these heavy metals in this study were 0.06 ng, 0.6 ng, 0.3 ng, 1.5 ng and 6 ng, respectively. 0.1 μg of each of heavy metal ions were added to N-CDs. The results showed that the coexistion of pharmaceutic excipients and metal ions did not interfere detection to Baicalin and RA by N-CDs (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Baicalin content in Baicalin capsules
ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing concentration of Baicalin in N-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually decreased (Fig. 6 ). The fluorescence quenching degree of N-CDs showed a linear relationship with the Baicalin concentration at the range of 1.6–72 μg·mL−1, the linear equation was ⊿F = 3.6861C + 0.2967(⊿F = F0 − Fx), r = 0.9992. The detection limit was obtained via dividing the slope of the standard curve by 3-fold standard deviations of the blank solution (n = 11), and its value was 1.0 μg·mL−1. A determination test was conducted (n = 9) using 0.9649 mg·mL−1 Baicalin reference solution (100 μL), with a relative standard deviation of 0.2%.

The fluorescence quenching degree of N-CDs increased with increasing concentration of reference Chinese herbal SR extract containing Baicalin, Baicalein, Wogonoside and Wogonin (Fig. 8 ). In the range of 5.04–201.6 μg·mL−1 and 201.6–352.8 μg·mL−1, the fluorescence quenching degree of N-CDs showed a piecewise linear relationship with the concentration of reference Chinese herbal SR extract. The linear equations are ⊿F = 1.8574C + 27.37, r = 0.998 (curve 1) and ⊿F = 0.7678C + 242.76, r = 0.997 (curve 2), respectively, ⊿F = F0 − Fx (Fig. 8). According to above described test method (see Section 3.3.2), the detection limits of two curves were 3.1 μg·mL−1 and 7.5 μg·mL−1, respectively, the relative standard deviation was 0.3% via a test using 100 μL of 3.024 mg·mL−1 reference Chinese herbal SR extract.

SR contains above four active components, so it also can quench the fluorescence of N-CDs. A method for the content determination of SR based on N-CDs fluorescence quenching could be established. The extract solution of SR (100 μL) was added into a 5 mL centrifuge tube containing 200 μL of N-CDs solution, followed by adding distilled water to the volume of 3 mL and shaking well. The fluorescence intensity Fx was measured and the quantification was conducted based on a standard curve. As a standard control, the content of the filtrate of reference Chinese herbal SR extract was measured using the same method. The contents of SR and standard control were 3.088 mg·mL−1 and 3.105 mg·mL−1, respectively. According to t-test, the results were not significant difference (t = 1.07, df = 5, p = 2.57, Table 3 ), indicated that SR showed the similar quality to reference Chinese herbal SR.

Due to many active components in Chinese medicinal herbs, it is not completely scientific and reasonable to evaluate the overall quality of medicinal herbs only according to the content of index components. In this study, the total content of reference Chinese herbal Scutellariae Radix extract as a standard control and SR extract was determined by fluorescence quenching of N-CDs, respectively. A method for evaluating overall quality of SR was established, providing a new way to conveniently and effectively evaluate the quality of Chinese medicinal herbs.

In this study, a fluorescence quenching system composed of the N-CDs, Baicalin and its derivatives was established, which was used to evaluate the quality of SR and determine the content of Baicalin in Baicalin capsule. The new detection method reduced pollution and time-consuming, could be quickly and effectively used in real SR sample. The further research on application of the fluorescence quenching system to other Chinese medicinal herbs is under way.

Weiqing Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Shuihua Wu: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Software. Daifang Wang: Formal analysis, Validation. Yongming Lou: Formal analysis, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the central government guide local special projects for science and technology development from Science and Technology Department of Fujian Province (2020L3018); Middle-aged and youth project of Education Department of Fujian Province (JAT191332); Biomedical health care innovation team of Fujian Vocational College of Bioengineering (2020TD01).

Chin. J. Pathophysiol.
